scenario: Strait of Hormuz de facto closure - P&I war risk withdrawal, tanker re-routing via Cape of Good Hope

categories.label: Food & Agriculture
categories.supplyChainImpact: Middle East urea and DAP fertilizer exports transit Hormuz, supplying ~30% of Asia's planted-area inputs. Shipping delays of 10-14 days via Cape route raise input costs and threaten planting windows for Vietnam, Thailand, and Indonesia rice belts. Palm oil - Asia's dominant cooking oil - faces surging logistics costs as Malaysia and Indonesia divert vessels.

assets.name: Wheat
assets.narrative: Fertilizer cost pass-through: Gulf urea +92% since Hormuz closure raises planting costs across Asia rice belts. Egypt (Hormuz-exposed wheat buyer) panic-buying elevates global benchmark. Source: Barchart ZWK26, StockPil Mar 2026.

assets.name: Corn
assets.narrative: Biofuel demand spike (SE Asia) as jet fuel prices surge. Indonesian ethanol mandate acceleration competes with feed use. Fertilizer cost uplift on next planting cycle.

assets.name: Soybeans
assets.narrative: Bunker cost spike (+42% on Asia-origin routes) squeezes South American soybean competitiveness. Indonesian and Vietnamese crush margins under pressure from elevated freight. Limited direct Hormuz exposure - US/South America origin - caps upside vs energy commodities.

assets.name: Palm Oil (CPO)
assets.narrative: Crude oil correlation driving CPO as biodiesel feedstock demand surges (Indonesia B40 mandate). Fertilizer disruption threatening next Indonesian yield cycle. Primary cooking oil substitute for disrupted Gulf edible-oil flows. Source: Bursa Malaysia BMD, Agropost closing prices Mar 24.

assets.name: Rice (Thai 25% FOB)
assets.narrative: ASEAN food security alarm: fertilizer shortfall threatens Vietnam and Thailand rice belts. Philippines, Indonesia importing at distressed freight premiums. India export ban in place since 2023 limits relief.

categories.label: Petrochemicals & Plastics
categories.supplyChainImpact: SABIC (Saudi), QAPCO (Qatar), and OCI (Oman) are among the world's largest petrochemical exporters - all ship via Hormuz. SE Asian crackers in Singapore, Thailand, and Malaysia rely on naphtha feedstock sourced from Gulf condensate. Downstream plastics, packaging, and agrochemicals face double squeeze: higher feedstock costs and logistics disruption.

assets.name: Naphtha (CFR Japan)
assets.narrative: Gulf condensate-derived naphtha - primary cracker feedstock for Singapore and Thailand - faces Hormuz blockade premium. Alternative Atlantic Basin supply adds 18-day shipping lag. Jurong Island and Map Ta Phut crackers running at reduced rates. Source: SGX NJF1 futures, Alkagesta March 2026.

assets.name: Ethylene (CFR NE Asia)
assets.narrative: Downstream from naphtha crunch. SABIC and QAPCO ethylene exports bottlenecked. SE Asian polyethylene (PE) and polyvinyl chloride (PVC) producers face margin compression. Agricultural film and irrigation pipe lead times extending 6-8 weeks. Source: ICE/ICIS CFR NE Asia, Syntex America Hormuz Week 3.

assets.name: Methanol (CFR China)
assets.narrative: Iran is world's 2nd largest methanol exporter - production disrupted directly by conflict. Qatar also a major exporter, force majeure in effect. China methanol-to-olefins (MTO) complex facing feedstock squeeze; acetic acid and formaldehyde chains disrupted. Partial offset from US and Chinese domestic supply limits crisis premium vs other petrochemicals. Source: Investing.com CFR China futures.

categories.label: Fertilizers
categories.supplyChainImpact: The Strait of Hormuz is the critical export artery for Middle East fertilizers: Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Oman, and UAE together account for ~18% of global urea supply and ~12% of DAP. QatarEnergy (Qafco) - world's largest urea exporter - declared force majeure on Mar 2. Iran is also a significant potash and urea producer. Asian planting windows create an acute near-term demand cliff.

assets.name: Urea (FOB Middle East)
assets.narrative: Qafco (Qatar) force majeure in effect since Mar 2. Saudi SAFCO and SABIC urea plants operating at ~60% capacity due to security precautions. Indonesian and Vietnamese rice planting calendars under acute threat. Egypt and India panic-buying from alternative (Russia/China) suppliers at large premiums. Iran rejection of peace plan on Mar 26 drove today's +8.2% surge. Source: CNBC Mar 25, DTN Mar 25, Investing.com FOB ME futures.

assets.name: DAP (FOB US Gulf)
assets.narrative: Saudi Ma'aden DAP - significant Asian supplier - shipping disrupted. US Gulf filling gap at premium freight. Philippines and Vietnam (high DAP users for rice) facing import cost spikes ahead of wet-season planting cycle.

assets.name: Potash (MOP CFR SE Asia)
assets.narrative: Less directly Hormuz-exposed (Canada, Russia main suppliers) but elevated freight costs add indirect pressure. Palm oil plantations (Indonesia, Malaysia) are major users - any yield disruption amplifies CPO supply concern.

categories.label: Shipping & Freight
categories.supplyChainImpact: P&I club war risk coverage withdrawal (effective March 5) triggered mass tanker re-routing via Cape of Good Hope, adding 10-14 transit days and absorbing ~8% of global VLCC fleet capacity. Container lines have activated war risk surcharges and re-routed services, extending Asia-Europe transit times. Bunker fuel costs elevated by crude spike compound freight economics.

assets.name: Baltic Dry Index
assets.narrative: Cape re-routing absorbing dry bulk vessel capacity as iron ore, coal, and grain shipments divert around southern Africa. Australia-Japan coal now routing Cape, adding 12 days. SE Asian rice and fertilizer import demand competing for Handysize and Supramax vessels. Source: Baltic Exchange via Hellenic Shipping News, Mar 25.

assets.name: VLCC AG-Asia Rate
assets.narrative: Very Large Crude Carriers on Arabian Gulf-Asia route remain at extreme levels; peaked WS 700+ on Mar 2-3 before moderation as Cape routing absorbed demand. Iran peace plan rejection on Mar 26 reversed the pullback. ~35% of VLCC fleet war-risk excluded from Gulf operations. ASEAN refiners paying distressed rates to maintain crude supply. Source: Seatrade Maritime, Lloyd's List, Roic.ai TCE data.

assets.name: Container Freight (SCFI)
assets.narrative: Asia-Europe container rates surging as Suez Canal services suspended and vessels divert Cape. Asia-Middle East lane disrupted - Dubai and Jeddah port congestion severe. SE Asian manufacturers (electronics, garments, furniture) facing extended lead times into European and Middle East markets. Source: Seoul Economic Daily Mar 14, Maersk Hormuz surcharge notice.
